Muscle	  spindles/golgi	  tendon	   organs	   o Cutaneous	  receptors	   • The	  basic	  circuit:	   o Sensory	  receptor	   o Sensory	  nerve	   o Interneurons	  –	  complexity	  here	  changes	  the	  nature	  of	  reflex.	   o Motor	  nerve	   o Muscle	  action	   • Hierarchy	  of	  reflexes	   o Control	  of	  individual	  muscles	   o Control	  of  and	  cortex)	   	   Control	  of	  a	  single	  muscle	   Monosynaptic	  stretch	  reflex	  (Tendon	  tap	  reflex)	  	   • Two	  neuron	  circuit	   • Negative	  feedback	  mechanism	  for	  maintaining	  muscle	  length.	   • 1a	  afferent	  (from	  muscle	  spindle)	  synapses	  with	  α	  motor	   neuron	  in	  ventral	  horn	   • A	  fast	  reflex	  response:	   o 1a	  afferent	  &	  α	  efferent:	  large	  diameter	  and	   myelinated	  	   o Single	  synapse	  ∴	  short	  synaptic	  delay	   	   	   	   	   Reflex control of muscle activity Stereotyped motor response to a sensory stimulus. – Muscle receptors – spindles and golgi tendon organs – Cutaneous receptors Reflex circuitry: Three levels of hierarchy in reflex circuitry: 1. Control of individual muscles 2. Control of muscles around a joint 3. Coordination of muscles at several joints Also activated by descending inputs from brain stem and cortex. The monosynaptic stretch reflex Two neuron circuit: single excitatory synaptic connection between Ia afferent fibre from a muscle spindle and an alpha motor neuron. Provides a negative feedback mechanism for regulating muscle length. Ia afferent = large diameter nerve, fast CV Single synapse = short synaptic delay Control of a single muscle 	   Inverse	  myotactic	  reflex	  	   • Provides	  a	  negative	  feedback	  for	  regulating	   muscle	  tension	  (protective	  function)	   o Eg	  heavy	  object	  suddenly	  on	  arms.	   Arms	  muscles	  will	  relax	  to	  protect	   self.	   • 1b	  afferent	  synapses	  with	  a	  1b	  inhibitory	   interneuron	  that	  synapses	  with	  α	  afferent	  in	   ventral	  horn.	   o 2	  synapses,	  thus	  longer	  latency	   o Interneuron	  is	  under	  control	  of	  upper	   centres	  ∴	  possible	  regulation	   	   The	  feedback	  loop	   • Initiated	  when	  there	  is	  a	  disturbance	  between	  the	  forces	  being	  produced	  by	  the	  muscle	  and	  length	  or	   tension	  change	  (causes	  firing	  of	  muscle	  spindle/golgi	  tendon	  organ)	   • The	  sensitivity	  of	  the	  loop	  can	  be	  regulated.	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Gain	  (modulating	  sensitivity)	   • The	  strength,	  sensitivity	  or	  effectiveness	  of	  the	  loop	   • Modified	  by:	   o Inputs	  to	  motoneurons	    Supraspinal	  fibres	  converge	  on	  the	  LMN	  to	  increase/decrease	  its	  excitability	  and	  thus	  the	   sensitivity	  of	  the	  feedback	  loop	  depending	  on	  need.	   • Important	  for	  voluntary	  movements	  where	  fine	  control	  may	  be	  required.	    Cutaneous	  receptors	   • Provide	  feedback	  to	  inhibitory	  interneurons,	  to	  control	  MN	  response	   o Fusimotor	  activity	    γ	  motor	  neurons	  regulate	  the	  sensitivity	  of	  the	  intrafusal	  receptors	  (muscle	  spindle)	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   The inverse myotactic reflex Requires an inhibitory interneuron (i.e. not monosynaptic! extra synaptic delay) Stimulation of 1b afferent from a golgi tendon organ causes reflex inhibition of the motorneurons via 1b inhibitory interneuron. Provides a negative feedback mechanism for regulating muscle tension. Control of a single muscle 	   Coordination	  about	  a	  joint	   • Muscle	  spindle	  afferent	  diverge	  in	  spinal	  cord	  to	  motoneurons	  and/or	  interneurons	  to	  stimulate/inhibit	   specific	  muscles,	  creating	  the	  desired	  muscle	  movement.	   • Basic	  pattern	  	   o excitation	  of	  same	  muscle	  (ie	  the	  muscle	  which	  provided	  the	  sensory	  signals)	   o excitation	  of	  synergist	  muscle/s	  (to	  assist	  the	  original	  muscle)	  	   o inhibition	  of	  antagonist	  muscle/s	   • e.g.	  1a	  afferent	  of	  the	  bicep	  will	  diverge	  in	  the	  spinal	  cord	  to	  a	  brachialis	  neuron,	  exciting	  it	  and	  ∴	   creating	  a	  synergy	  between	  the	  two	  in	  the	  movement.	  But,	  the	  1a	  afferent	  would	  also	  synapse	  with	  an	   inhibitory	  interneuron	  to	  inhibit/relax	  the	  triceps	  –	  making	  flexion	  easier.	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Interneurons	   • 1a	  interneuron	  for	  1a	  afferent	  (intrafusal)	   • 1b	  interneuron	  for	  1b	  afferent	  (golgi	  tendon)	   o Synapses	  with	  other	  neurons	  for	  further	  regulation	   • Renshaw	  Cell	  –	  has	  many	  divergent	  outputs.	   o Motoneuron	  sends	  a	  collateral	  to	  a	  Renshaw	  cell,	  the	   Renshaw	  cell	  in	  turn	  inhibits	  the	  same	  MN	    Dampens	  the	  firing	  rate	  of	  the	  MN	    Regulate	  smoothness	  and	  strength	  of	  contraction	   o Also	  synapses	  with	  interneurons	  to	  increase	  the	  excitability	   of	  the	  antagonist	  MN	  by	  inhibiting	  the	  interneurons	   inhibiting	  them	  (disinhibition)	   	   Overall	  response	  depends	  on	  summation	  of	  convergent	  sensory	  input:	   afferents	  (1a,	  1b,	  cutaneous,	  joint	  receptors),	  interneurons,	  high	  control	   centres	  (descending	  pathways)	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Renshaw Cell Another inhibitory interneurone Divergent connections to MN’s and IN’s that affect muscles about the joint. MN’s send collaterals to Renshaw cells causing inhibition of the same MN’s = recurrent inhibition. Stabilises MN firing by counteracting large transient changes in firing rate. Inhibits inhibitory IN’s = disinhibition It’s own activity is also modified by descending inputs. 	   Polysynaptic	  reflex	  -­‐	  Cutaneous	  stimuli	  causes	  complex	  multi	  joint	   reflexes	  	   	   Flexion	  withdrawal	  reflex	   • Nociceptive	  stimuli	  causes	  activation	  of	  flexor	  muscles	  and	   inhibition	  of	  extensor	  muscles	  causing	  the	  leg	  to	  be	  raised	   • Experience	  co-­‐ordinated	  contraction	  of	  multiple	  joints	   	   	   	   Crossed	  extensor	  reflex	  	   • The	  nociceptive	  stimuli	  interacts	  with	  interneurons	  that	   stimulates	  contralateral	  leg	  extension	  and	  inhibition	  of	  flexor	   muscles	  this	  providing	  us	  a	  leg	  to	  support	  ourselves	  on	  or	  a	   method	  of	  pushing	  the	  body	  further	  away	  from	  the	  painful	   stimuli.	   It	  is	  also	  possible	  to	  call	  on	  these	  pre-­‐programmed	  pathways	  for	   normal	  activities	  (ie	  something	  else	  could	  stimulate	  the	  pathways)	  	   	   	   Interneurons	  Summary	  	   • Control	  complex	  motor	  responses	  to	  various	  sensory	  input	  	   o more	  interneurons	  =	  more	  complex	  response	   • Divergent	  connection	  –	  single	  sensory	  activates	  multiple	  MN	   • Convergence	  –	  MN	  receives	  multiple	  stimulating	  or	  inhibitory	  responses.	   • Descending	  Upper	  Neurons	  can	  inhibit	  or	  allow	  the	  transmission	  of	  a	  signal	  from	  an	  (inter)neuron	  to	  the	   next	  neuron	  (Gating)	   o Two	  forms:	    Inhibit	  the	  postsynaptic	  (inter)neuron,	  reducing	  it’s	  excitability	    Inhibit	  the	  presynaptic	  (inter)neuron,	  preventing/reducing	  nt	  release.	   • Rhythmic	  alternating	  activity	   o Two	  descending	  pathways	  synapse	  with	  a	  flexor/extensor	  interneuron	   o Interneurons	  stimulate	  motoneuron	  &	  send	  a	  collateral	  to	  inhibit	  the	  other	  interneuron.	   o Due	  to	  synaptic	  delays,	  an	  alternating	  rhythmical	  activity	  of	  action	  potentials	  develops	  in	  the	   motoneurons.	  	   	   	   Clinical	   Muscle	  Tone	   • Force	  with	  which	  muscle	  resists	  being	  lengthened	   o Determined	  by	  the	  number	  of	  activated	  motor	  units.	   o Can	  be	  due	  to	  elastic	  stiffness	  due	  to	  proteins	  eg	  titin	  and	  desmin	   • Tested	  by	  passive	  movement	   • Neural	  influences	  increase	  muscle	  tone	   o Hypertonus,	  spasticity,	  clonus	  affects	  Gain	   	   Tendon	  Tap	   • Quick	  stretch	  (dynamic	  length	  change)	   • Observe	  response	  for	  hyper/hypoactive	   • Hypoactive	  –	  low	  gain	  of	  the	  reflex	  pathway	   o Due	  to	  damage	  in	  LMNs	   o Decreased	  muscle	  tone	   • Hyperactive	  –	  increased	  gain	   o Due	  to	  damaged	  UMNs.	  	  Withdrawal	  of	  inhibitory	  influences	   • Babinski	  Sign	  (cutaneous	  reflex)	   o Extensor	  plantar	  response	  –	  extension	  of	  big	  toe/fanning	  of	  other	  toes	  instead	  of	  flexion	  indicates	   lesion	  in	  the	  corticospinal	  tract	   • Spasticity	   o Hypertonus	  due	  to	  UMN	  lesion	  whereby	  the	  muscle	  shows	  abnormally	  high	  resistance	  to	  stretch,	   often	  accompanied	  by	  clonus	  (rapid	  contractions	  and	  relaxation	  in	  response	  to	  stretch)	  	   Summary: Interneurones allow for more complex motor behaviour by being interposed between sensory and motor neurones. The reflexes are therefore polysynaptic. The important spinal inhibitory interneurones are the Ia, Ib and Renshaw cells Interneurons are the building blocks of spinal reflexes
